mnemonics.title: Permutation vs Combination
mnemonics.content: PERMUTATION = ARRANGEMENT (ordered). COMBINATION = SELECTION (unordered). Remember: 'Permutation sounds like Position (order matters), Combination sounds like Committee (just select, no order).'

mnemonics.title: Circular Permutation Formula
mnemonics.content: For circular arrangements, remove 1: (n-1)! This is because fixing one position eliminates rotation symmetry. Remember: 'One position fixed, rest are free!'

mnemonics.title: Binomial Sum Identity
mnemonics.content: Sum of all C(n,r) = 2^n. Remember: 'One and One make Two, so sum is Two to the n!' (from (1+1)^n).

acronyms.expansion: Permutation of n objects taken r at a time
acronyms.usage: Used when order matters: n!/(n-r!) = nPr

acronyms.expansion: Combination of n objects taken r at a time
acronyms.usage: Used when order doesn't matter: n!/(r!(n-r)!) = nCr
If A can be done m ways and B can be done n ways, together: m×n ways

visualTips.visualization: Draw a triangle with 1s on both edges. Each number is sum of two numbers above it: Row 0: 1, Row 1: 1 1, Row 2: 1 2 1, Row 3: 1 3 3 1. Row n gives all C(n,k) values.
Think of arranging books on a shelf. Each position matters - first book, second book, etc. Visualize slots: _ _ _ where each slot gets one book in different orders.
Think of selecting a team from players. Once selected, the team is the same regardless of order. Visualize picking from a group where only 'who's in' matters, not 'who's first'.
For distributing n identical items into k boxes: Draw n stars (∗) and k-1 bars (|). Count positions to place bars among stars. Example: **|*|** means 2,1,2.

analogies.analogy: Like arranging people in a photo - position matters. A line (A,B,C) is different from (C,B,A). Similar to ranking in a race where 1st, 2nd, 3rd places matter.
Like choosing team members - once chosen, the team is the same regardless of order. Picking 3 players from 10 for a team: only who gets picked matters, not who was picked first.
Like seating people around a round table where only relative position matters. Rotating the entire arrangement doesn't create a new seating - only swapping neighbors creates difference.
Like Pascal's triangle building blocks - each number is supported by two numbers below it. The middle numbers are largest because they have the most combinations.
Like locker combinations or passwords - characters can repeat. Each position is independent with same set of choices. Example: 4-digit PIN has 10^4 possibilities.
